aliases: 穆尔塔巴肉馅饼 (Mùěr Tǎbā Ròuxiàn Bǐng)

chinese: 穆尔塔巴肉馅饼 (Mùěr Tǎbā Ròuxiàn Bǐng)

region_of_origin: Singapore (Arab-Indian heritage via Hadhramaut traders, Yemen)
food_subgroup: Stuffed pan-fried flatbread — thick layered wheat dough filled with spiced minced mutton, egg, and onion, pan-fried in ghee

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Murtabak. Closest parent is stuffed flatbread (FOODON:03315763) or meat-filled pancake. No exact URI assigned; gap documented.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pancakes and similar products — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for stuffed pan-fried flatbread. Murtabak is a filled, pan-fried wheat-dough product analogous to stuffed pancake category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Murtabak. A0CKP (Pancakes and similar products) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Murtabak or sufficiently close stuffed flatbread equivalent.

serving.description: 1 large murtabak with curry dipping broth
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore hawker serving of mutton murtabak is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 280 g of stuffed wheat bread parcel plus 100 g of spiced mutton filling and 20 g of curry dipping broth, yielding approximately 840 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Murtabak Mutton — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Murtabak Mutton. HPB Foodo is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas source policy. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this source. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Refined Wheat Flatbread / Roti-type Products — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (www.glycemicindex.com) — analogous estimate based on refined wheat flatbread (chapati/roti-type, GI ~62)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Murtabak in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 (medium) is estimated by analogy to refined wheat flatbread / roti-type products (GI range 57–70 in University of Sydney GI Database). The murtabak dough is a refined wheat product similar in composition to roti canai / chapati. The protein and fat from the mutton-egg filling moderates the overall glycemic response relative to plain bread. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (62 × 80 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 49.6 GL (very high). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg/100 g; ~1,400 mg per 400 g serving — approximately 70% of WHO 2,000 mg daily limit) combined with high saturated fat from ghee (5 g/100 g; ~20 g per serving, equalling 100% of recommended daily saturated fat intake)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid regular consumption of mutton murtabak; if consumed occasionally, request oil-fried rather than ghee-fried preparation and limit to a half-portion (200 g) to reduce sodium intake to approximately 700 mg and saturated fat to approximately 10 g per eating occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per standard serving (~50 GL) driven by approximately 80 g available carbohydrate from refined wheat dough (GI ~62); one full portion delivers more carbohydrate than two cups of cooked white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should not consume a full murtabak portion as a regular meal; if eaten on a special occasion, sharing one portion between two people reduces the glycemic load to approximately 25 GL, and the protein and fat from the mutton-egg filling provides partial glycemic buffering compared to equivalent plain bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~20 g per 400 g serving, 100% of daily recommended limit) from ghee frying, combined with 110 mg cholesterol per 100 g (~440 mg per serving) and high sodium (1,400 mg per serving), all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat mutton murtabak as an occasional food only; choosing oil-fried over ghee-fried preparation and limiting portion to 150–200 g can meaningfully reduce saturated fat and cholesterol exposure per eating occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton filling provides iron (2.2 mg/100 g) and zinc (2.5 mg/100 g) — anti-inflammatory cofactors relevant to RA — but the high saturated fat load from ghee is pro-inflammatory and may exacerbate systemic inflammation at full serving size
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume a small portion of mutton murtabak occasionally for its iron and zinc content, but should prefer oil-fried preparation and limit portion size to reduce pro-inflammatory saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murtabak (مُرَتَّبَق) is Singapore's most celebrated Indian Muslim hawker dish: a massive, thin-stretched refined wheat dough filled with spiced minced mutton, beaten egg, and diced onion, then pan-fried in ghee on a large iron griddle until the exterior is crispy and golden while the interior remains soft, savoury, and richly flavoured. The finished parcel is cut into rectangular squares and served with a light curry broth for dipping. The name derives from the Arabic word 'mutabbaq' (meaning 'folded'), and the dish arrived in Southeast Asia via Hadhramaut traders from Yemen, spreading through Indian Muslim (Mamak) communities across the region. In Singapore, murtabak is most famously associated with Arab Street and historic establishments such as Zam Zam Restaurant (founded 1908) and Hajjah Maimunah, but is now available at Indian Muslim hawker stalls throughout the island. It should not be confused with the sweet Indonesian martabak manis, which is an entirely different stuffed pancake. Nutritionally, mutton murtabak is one of the most calorie- and carbohydrate-dense single-dish hawker foods in Singapore: a standard 400 g serving contains approximately 840 kcal, 80 g carbohydrate, 44 g fat (including 20 g saturated fat from ghee), 36 g protein, and approximately 1,400 mg sodium. The glycemic load per serving is approximately 50 GL — among the highest of any Singapore hawker dish — making it unsuitable as a regular meal for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Despite its nutritional density, murtabak is a culturally important festive and late-night food, typically consumed as a special occasion meal rather than a daily staple. The mutton filling contributes meaningful iron (2.2 mg/100 g), zinc (2.5 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (0.8 mcg/100 g).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo — the approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is estimated by analogy to refined wheat flatbread from the University of Sydney GI Database (confidence 0.9) as no direct GI measurement for murtabak exists in any approved GI source. All mandatory multilingual names are present. All hard gates pass.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for murtabak mutton not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this dish. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — ghee-fried preparation does not introduce industrial trans fats; naturally occurring ruminant trans fats are below detection threshold in this formulation). GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat flatbread / roti-type products from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singapore murtabak exists in any approved source. Glycemic load of 49.6 GL is calculated as (62 × 80 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100. The input record listed multiple portions with is_default: true; the canonical default serving is set to 400 g (280 g stuffed bread parcel + 100 g filling + 20 g curry broth) as the most commonly cited standard Singapore hawker portion. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pancakes and similar products) is used as the closest defensible parent category; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for murtabak.
